text: Aaron Hernandez, the former New England Patriots tight end, has been convicted of first-degree murder in the 2013 slaying of Odin Lloyd. Despite a lack of concrete evidence linking him to the murder, Hernandez appeared confident and unapologetic throughout the trial. He did not offer a plausible alibi, and his own attorney admitted that he witnessed the murder but did not commit it. Hernandez was sentenced to life without the possibility of parole and was also found guilty of unlawful possession of a firearm and unlawful possession of ammunition. Many believe that Hernandez's arrogance in court and his failure to show remorse were major factors in his conviction.

text: Aaron Hernandez, a former New England Patriot, was convicted of first-degree murder and sentenced to life in prison without parole. Despite his arrogant demeanor in court, the jury found him guilty of witnessing the killing of Odin Lloyd, a friend, committed by someone he knew. Hernandez's troubled past, including bar fights and failed drug tests, was revealed before he was drafted by the Patriots in 2010. However, the NFL and the Patriots handled the situation correctly by releasing him hours after he was charged and not trying to defend him as a good person. The case highlights the difficulty in predicting the actions of individuals, even with extensive background checks, and the importance of holding perpetrators accountable for their crimes.

Despite the league's efforts to vet its players, sometimes even the best can go wrong, and Hernandez is an anomaly in the otherwise law-abiding league.

text: A 37-year-old man named Louis Jordan survived 66 days at sea after his boat capsized during a storm, breaking his mast and his shoulder. When his food and water supplies ran out, Jordan survived by catching fish that were attracted to his laundry, which he used to rinse in the sea. He was eventually found hundreds of miles from land by the second mate of the Houston Express, a 1,000-foot long container ship. Jordan was in surprisingly good condition when found, according to Capt. Thomas Grenz. Coast Guard officials have found no reason to doubt Jordan's incredible story of survival. Jordan is an unemployed truck driver who lived on his boat at a marina in Conway, South Carolina. When it became difficult to catch dinner, he took off for the ocean in hopes he would land some bigger fish. Despite being an inexperienced sailor, Jordan's father said he held hope because his son had a good boat and a strong constitution.

Louis Jordan, a 37-year-old man from Conway, South Carolina, was found alive after spending 66 days lost at sea. Jordan had set out on a fishing trip in his 35-foot sailboat, but a storm caused the boat to capsize and his mast to break. With a broken shoulder, Jordan was unable to fix the boat and was left drifting in the ocean. After enduring multiple capsizes and storms, Jordan's food and water eventually ran out, and he struggled to collect fresh water due to the rough conditions. However, he managed to attract fish using his laundry, which he would put out to sea for rinsing. Jordan was eventually spotted by the crew of the Houston Express, a container ship, and was rescued. Despite his ordeal, Jordan was in surprisingly good condition when found and had not lost much weight or suffered severe sunburns. Coast Guard officials confirmed his incredible story and noted that his father had reported him and his boat missing on January 29. Jordan's father praised his son's strength and spirituality, saying that he had prayed throughout his ordeal. Jordan is expected to return to his boat in the near future.
